coredata.dc:title: Visible-light excitable thermally activated delayed fluorescence in aqueous solution from F, N-doped carbon dots confined in silica nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Mo, Luoqi
Xu, Xiaokai
Liu, Zhiming
Liu, Hao
Lei, Bingfu
Zhuang, Jianle
Guo, Zhouyi
Liu, Yingliang
Hu, Chaofan
coredata.dc:description: Visible light has better biocompatibility and is more accessible for anti-counterfeiting compared to UV light. However, the synthesis of visible light excitable CDs-based long afterglow materials is still a challenge, especially in aqueous solution. Herein, we provide the first example of visible-light excitable thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) in aqueous solution by confining the fluorine and nitrogen co-doped CDs in silica nanoparticles (F, NCDs@SiO2). The hydrogen network and covalent bonds effectively protect the triplet excitons from quenching by water oxygen, so the nanoparticles can produce long-lived TADF in aqueous solutions under the excitation of white light. Meanwhile, the large number of silanol groups on the surface of amorphous silicon endows F, NCDs@SiO2 with water solubility making them possible for time-resolved probes. We reveal that fluorine doping is essential for obtaining visible-light excitable TADF, which redshifts the excitation spectra and narrows the energy level between the singlet and triplet states through strong electron-withdrawing effect and heavy-atom effects. Our work provides a new strategy for the development of visible-light-excited TADF materials in aqueous solution and potential applications of CDs in information encryption, temperature sensor and time-resolved fluorescence bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Visible-light-excited
Carbon dots
Thermally activated delayed fluorescence
Bioimaging
Temperature sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 21571067 , 51402207 , 11874021 , and 32071399 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province ( 2020A1515010443 ), and the Science and Technology Program of Guangzhou ( 2019050001 )

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Schematic illustration of the preparation procedure for F, NCDs@SiO2 and the photographs of F, NCDs@SiO2 in aqueous solution after cell phone LED excitation. b-c) Schematic illustration of the possible structural formation (b) and delayed fluorescence (c) of F, NCDs@SiO2.

a) TEM image (insert showing particles size distribution), b) HR-TEM image, c) FFT diffraction pattern of the F, NCDs@SiO2. d) HR-TEM image, e) TEM image (insert: particles size distribution) of F, NCDs. f) FT-IR spectra of F, NCDs, F, NCDs@SiO2 and SiO2, respectively. g-k) XPS spectra of full scan (g), C 1 s (h), O 1 s (i), , F 1 s (j) and Si 2p (k) of F, NCDs@SiO2.

a) The normalized fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of the F, NCNDs@SiO2. b) Afterglow spectra of solid-state F, NCDs@SiO2 at different temperatures under excitation of 460 nm. c) The corresponding delayed fluorescence intensity compared with temperature dependence (solid line: fitting). d) Photographs of F, NCDs@SiO2 aqueous solution and powder after flashlight irradiation (10 W). e) Gaussian fittings of the phosphorescence emission spectra of F, NCDs@SiO2 under excitation of 460 nm. f) 3D phosphorescence plots of F, CDs@SiO2. g) Afterglow decay curve and lifetimes of aqueous F, NCDs@SiO2 monitored at 500 nm. h) The UV − Vis absorption and excitations of fluorescence and afterglow spectra of F, NCDs@SiO2 solution monitored at 500 nm.

a) The fluorescence spectra of the F, NCDs@SiO2 and NCDs@SiO2. b) The UV − Vis absorption, and the excitations of fluorescence (λem = 410) and afterglow (λem = 425, 470 nm) spectra of aqueous NCDs@SiO2. c) The afterglow excitation spectra of F, NCDs@SiO2 and NCDs @ SiO2. d) Phosphorescent emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths of aqueous NCDs@SiO2 (inset: photographs were taken before and after the UV light). e) Gaussian fitting of the afterglow emission spectra of NCDs@SiO2 with the excitation of 355 nm. f) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of NCDs@SiO2 at the low temperature (77 K) under excitation at 355 nm. g) Proposed possible afterglow mechanism of F, NCDs@SiO2 and NCDs@SiO2 in aqueous solution.
a-b) Information encryption (a) and Graphic security (b) application based on F, NCDs@SiO2 under UV, and cell phone LED on and off, respectively.

a) CCK-8 assessed the cell viability of HepG2 cells incubated with F, NCDs@SiO2 for 24 h. b) phosphorescence emission intensities of F, NCDs@SiO2 solution under continuous excitation at 460 nm for two hours. c) Normalized fluorescence and phosphorescence intensities of aqueous F, NCDs@SiO2 under different pH values under excitation at 460 nm. d) The confocal images of HepG2 cells incubated with F, NCDs@SiO2 and F, NCDs solutions, including bright field, fluorescence images and delayed fluorescence images (time-gated at 8.5–12 ns).

head.ce:title: Visible-light excitable thermally activated delayed fluorescence in aqueous solution from F, N-doped carbon dots confined in silica nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510631, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education
Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Luoqi Mo and Xiaokai Xu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Visible light has better biocompatibility and is more accessible for anti-counterfeiting compared to UV light. However, the synthesis of visible light excitable CDs-based long afterglow materials is still a challenge, especially in aqueous solution. Herein, we provide the first example of visible-light excitable thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) in aqueous solution by confining the fluorine and nitrogen co-doped CDs in silica nanoparticles (F, NCDs@SiO2). The hydrogen network and covalent bonds effectively protect the triplet excitons from quenching by water oxygen, so the nanoparticles can produce long-lived TADF in aqueous solutions under the excitation of white light. Meanwhile, the large number of silanol groups on the surface of amorphous silicon endows F, NCDs@SiO2 with water solubility making them possible for time-resolved probes. We reveal that fluorine doping is essential for obtaining visible-light excitable TADF, which redshifts the excitation spectra and narrows the energy level between the singlet and triplet states through strong electron-withdrawing effect and heavy-atom effects. Our work provides a new strategy for the development of visible-light-excited TADF materials in aqueous solution and potential applications of CDs in information encryption, temperature sensor and time-resolved fluorescence bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Afterglow materials including room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) have gained increasing attention owing to their potential applications in the fields of optoelectronic devices, information encryption, biological probes, and optical sensing [1–4]. As biological probes and anti-counterfeiting inks, afterglow materials are required to have excellent water solubility, biocompatibility and optical properties. Traditional organometallic and pure organic materials for afterglow probes are expensive, toxicity and short afterglow lifetime are not conducive to practical applications [5–9]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a new afterglow material system to face these challenges.

In recent years, room temperature afterglow materials based on carbon dots (CDs) have received extensive attention due to their excellent optical properties, biocompatibility and raw material diversity [10–13]. The high efficiency of non-radiative transition, poor intersystem crossing (ISC) and the easy quenching by water and oxygen are the difficulties that must be faced in achieving room temperature afterglow [14]. To obtain room temperature afterglow, some polymer-like CDs with crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect [13,15,16] and carbon dots with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect [17] have been developed. Other than that, the vast majority of CDs-based afterglow comes from doping CDs into various substrates, such as polyvinyl alcohol [16], recrystallization urea [18], boric acid [19], cyanuric acid [20], zeolite frameworks [21], and amorphous silica [22,23]. The previous strategy mainly focused on the development of CDs-based long afterglow materials in solid-state because these rigid matrices can effectively stabilize the triplet state by resisting moisture and oxygen and restrict molecular vibrations to suppress non-radiative transitions. Nevertheless, several successful attempts to develop CDs-based long afterglow materials in aqueous solution have been reported that use cyanuric acid or colloidal silica as the matrices [20,22–24]. However, the excitation source for afterglow is limited to ultraviolet (UV) light. The UV light as the excitation light source will impede the further application in time-resolved imaging due to the shallow tissue penetration and cell toxicity of ultraviolet light. Meanwhile, these UV-light-excited afterglow materials are wildly used in anti-counterfeiting and easily to be mimicked. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop visible-light-excited CDs-based long afterglow materials in aqueous solution for anti-counterfeiting and bioimaging applications.

Fluorination of CDs has emerged as a potent strategy for fine-tuning their physical and optical properties [25–27]. Fluorine, the most electronegative atom, as a substituent in π-conjugated backbones can effectively reduce the energy level between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and enhance intermolecular charge transfer (ICT) [25,29,29]. Therefore, the excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs will tend to redshift after fluorination. Besides, as a traditional heavy atom, fluorine can effectively increase the ISC rate and promote spin–orbit coupling (SOC) via internal heavy atom effects [30]. Thus fluorine-doped CDs have the potential to develop visible light excitation or long-wavelength emission afterglow.

Herein, we encapsulated a kind of fluorine and nitrogen co-doped CDs (F, NCDs) with amorphous silica by the sol–gel method to obtained TADF materials in aqueous solution (F, NCDs@SiO2). The TADF of nanoparticles can be excited by cell phone LED, (Fig. 1
a and Supplementary Movie 1 ), which is convenient for its application in anti-counterfeiting and more secretive. Fig. 1 b shows that the CDs and the amorphous silica are connected by covalent bond and hydrogen networks to effectively protect triplet excitons and inhibit non-radiative transitions to produce long-lived afterglow. And we demonstrate that the fluorine doping is crucial for TADF production and visible light excitation by control experiments. Owning to the unique optical properties and water solubility, F, NCDs@SiO2 are used as anti-counterfeiting inks, temperature sensors and bioimaging probes.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) clearly shows that the F, NCDs exhibited good mono-dispersion with an average size of 2.98 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, matching the (100) facet of graphite (Fig. 2
d-e) [13,22]. To understand the nature of TADF materials, the morphology of F, NCDs@SiO2 was firstly characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The F, NCDs@SiO2 exhibit good dispersion and quasi-spherical shapes with an average particle size of 31.4 nm, while the CDs are distributed in amorphous silica (Fig. 2 a-b and Figure S1 ). Furthermore, the distribution of fluorine elements in the CDs overlaps well with the silicon elements, as shown in the TEM mapping images ( Figure S2 ). Meanwhile the Fast Fourier Transformation (FFT) diffraction pattern (Fig. 2 c) of the HR-TEM image of F, NCDs@SiO2 reveals there are many carbon lattice planes in it [31]. Those above discussion demonstrates that F, NCDs are immobilized in silica. The homogeneous structure of F, CDs@SiO2 may be responsible for their stability in water for a long time without precipitation and retention of afterglow ( Figure S1
5).

The chemical composition of F, NCDs and F, NCDs@SiO2 are followed identified by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2 f (blue line), a strong and wide absorption peak occurs at 2450–3300 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of -N–H and the -O–H stretching vibration of the carboxylic acid on the F, NCDs surface [20,32]. The peaks observed at 1033, 1132 and 1670 cm−1 are owing to the stretching vibration of semi-ionic C–F bonding, covalent C–F bonding and C = O, respectively [27,33,34]. After wrapping F, NCDs with amorphous silica, compared with the spectra of silica, a new characteristic absorption peak at 1245 cm−1 is generated by Si-C stretching vibration [23,24]. These characterization and analyses indicate that SiO2 generated in the water bath wrapped F, NCDs via Si-O covalent bonds. The XPS investigation of F, NCDs@SiO2 was also performed and compared with free F, NCDs ( Figure S3 ), to further confirm their composition. The XPS spectra (Fig. 2 g) reveals the presence of an emerging peak in F, NCDs@SiO2 compared to F, NCDs with C, N and O demonstrating the presence of Si in F, NCDs@SiO2. In Fig. 2 h, the high-resolution XPS spectra of the C 1 s band was separated into five peaks at 284.3, 285.0, 285.9, 286.6 and 288.5 eV, which are assigned to C–Si, C–C/C = C, C-O/C-N, C = O and C-F, respectively [24,32]. The high-resolution O 1 s spectra (Fig. 2 i) can be de-convoluted into C-O (532.0 eV), O = C-O (533.0 eV) and SiO2 (533.7 eV) peaks [24]. The F 1 s peak diagram shows two obvious peaks, 685.2 and 687.1 eV, corresponding to the semi-ionic C–F and covalent C–F bonding (Fig. 2 j) [33]. The Si 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 2 k) contain three components that can be assigned to Si-C (103.0 eV), Si-O-C (103.7 eV), and SiOx (104.5 eV) [35]. These characterizations and discussions above have proven that F, NCDs have fluorine groups with strong electronegative on their surfaces, which differs from the ordinary CDs reported in previous work, and that F, NCDs are protected in amorphous silica through covalent bonds.

Interestingly, the afterglow of the aqueous solution and powder of F, NCDs@SiO2 lasted for a few seconds with the excitation of flashlight (Fig. 3
d), which is the first example of visible-light excitable TADF in aqueous solution. The reported works about water-soluble afterglow nanoparticles are summarized, and the corresponding excitation wavelengths are shown in Table S1. All of the excitation wavelengths of water-soluble afterglow materials are in the UV region, while the excitation wavelength of the complex F, NCDs@SiO2 is in the visible region. In-depth characterization and discussion of the optical properties of F, NCDs@SiO2 are useful for the development of visible-light excitable afterglow materials based on CDs. Figure S4
a-b shows that the consistency of the photoluminescence (PL) spectra between F, NCDs and F, NCDs@SiO2 can confirm that the optical properties of the CDs are preserved after the silica encapsulated CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the afterglow spectra is similar to the instantaneous spectra of F, NCDs@SiO2 solution. And the emission spectra of F, NCDs@SiO2 for different time gating (from 1 to 5 ms) are also consistent with the prompt spectra, which is in accordance with the properties of TADF ( Figure S5 ). Note that the F, NCDs will not have any afterglow in solution. To detect the thermal effect of F, NCDs@SiO2 afterglow, temperature-dependent afterglow spectra were measured. As shown in Fig. 3 b, the afterglow emission at 500 nm was relatively obvious when the temperature reached 250 K and was stronger as the temperature increases, confirming the existence of a thermally assisted process. The triplet exciton accumulated at T1 returns to S1 via the RISC process requiring the help of thermal activation due to the higher energy level of S1 relative to T1 [36,37]. Importantly, Fig. 3 c shows that the afterglow intensity of the F, NCDs@SiO2 solution increases linearly when the temperature changes from 303 K to 353 K (R2 = 0.992), making F, NCDs@SiO2 possible to evaluate surrounding temperature. It is well known that low energy levels (ΔEST) between the singlet (S1) and triplet states (T1) favor the re-excitation of electrons from the T1 to the S1, generating TADF [12,38]. The ΔEST was calculated as 0.32 eV by measuring the FL and phosphorescence spectra of F, NCDs@SiO2 at low temperature (77 K) under excitation at 460 nm ( Figure S6 ) [35]. Such a small energy level is conducive for the electrons in T1 to reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) and then radiative transitions to the ground state, generating TADF. Fig. 3 e shows that the afterglow of F, NCDs is composed of TADF and phosphorescence, but TADF is the main component (about 70%) at room temperature by Gaussian fitting. Interestingly, the afterglow excitation wavelength is in the range of 440 nm to 480 nm and the optimum excitation wavelength locates at 460 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 f. The excitation wavelength of the afterglow is located in the visible region, so the F, NCDs solution can produce afterglow after being excited by white light. The time-resolved spectra of F, NCDs@SiO2 aqueous solution under excitation at 460 nm was measured to determine the TADF lifetimes. The decay spectra was fitted with triexponential functions well. On the base of the following equation (1):
(1)
τ
avg
= ∑
α i
τ
i
2
/ ∑
α i
τ i

the average lifetime was calculated to be 0.48 s (Fig. 3 g). There are three components of the lifetime that compose the lifetime of the afterglow of 1.04 s (1.88%), 0.61 s (32.44%) and 0.27 s (65.68%), respectively. This result implies that there are two decay channels mainly responsible for the observed afterglow emission (the 1.04 s (1.88%) component can be ignored). The 0.61 s (32.44%) is attributed to the decay of phosphorescence and 0.27 s (65.58%) is attributed to the decay of TADF, which ratios are consistent with the Gaussian fit of the afterglow spectra. Also, the absolute PL quantum yield (QY) of the F, NCDs@SiO2 solution was determined to be 58.8% using the integrating sphere at 460 nm excitation, which is higher than the free F, NCDs (54.3%) and the PL average decay time of F, NCDs@SiO2 (4.21 ns) is longer than that of F, NCDs (4.01 ns), as shown in Figure S7 . These indicate that the excitons are more stable in composite material. However, it is difficult to distinguish between the rapid and delayed PLQY due to the large difference between the two lifetimes [39,40]. The complex can be uniformly dispersed in water and achieve long-lived TADF due to the protection of the covalent bond and hydrogen network. CDs are immobilized in amorphous silica through covalent bonds, which effectively isolates environmental quenchers such as oxygen and water, and suppresses molecular motion. After degassing the F, NCDs@SiO2 solution, the emission intensity of TADF was significantly enhanced and the lifetime had a slight enhancement, as shown in Figure S8 . The temperature-dependent fluorescence spectra of F, NCDs@SiO2 ( Figure S9 ) shows fluorescence enhancement as the temperature increases from 100 K to 325 K, due to the promotion of stored photon escape [23]. Then, the fluorescence intensity decreases with temperature from 325 K to 425 K because of the violent molecular motion. It is demonstrated that oxygen and molecular motion have a strong quenching effect on excitons. Therefore, in order to obtain appreciable phosphorescence or TADF, it is necessary to isolate the emitting unit from oxygen and to inhibit molecular motion [41]. It is also important that fluorine with strong electron-withdrawing effect can form hydrogen networks to inhibit molecular movement. The above-mentioned factors effectively protect the triplet excitons to obtain long-lived afterglow. Meanwhile, the rich silanol groups on the surface of amorphous silica make the nanoparticles have good hydrophilicity.

The UV–Vis absorption and excitation spectra of F, NCDs@SiO2 solutions were examined to evaluate their emission sources. Firstly, since the optimal excitation wavelength of afterglow and fluorescence are both at 460 nm, we can infer that fluorescence and afterglow come from the same luminescent center. (Fig. 3 h) [42]. In addition, the UV − Vis absorption of the F, NCDs@SiO2 solution has two peaks at 285 nm and 460 nm, which are attributed to the π-π* transition of C = C and the n-π* transition of C = O/C = N, respectively [43,44]. Since the excitation center of the afterglow is consistent with the absorption of F, NCDs at 460 nm, we can infer that TADF originates from the C = O/C = N bonds in CDs. The electrons in the ground state transitions to the excited state after absorbing energy. One part of the electron returns to the S0 via radiative transitions to produce fluorescence, and one-part transitions from S1 to T1 via ISC. Thanks to the low energy levels (0.32 eV) between the S1 and T1, the excitons accumulated in the triplet state reverse ISC to S1 via thermal motion and then radiative transitions to the ground state to produce TADF, as shown in Fig. 1 c. Previously reported work has proved that C = O and C = N bonds are responsible for the generation of CDs-based afterglow by promoting ISC to the triplet states [44,45]. However, the absorption peak of C = O and C = N is mainly in the range of 320 nm-380 nm in CDs with afterglow, so the afterglow of most CDs can only be excited by UV. We speculate that the doping of fluorine leads to the redshift of the absorption peak to achieve visible-light excitable TADF. To confirm the validity of this assumption, fluorine-free nanoparticles (NCDs@SiO2) were synthesized.

The fluorine-free CDs (NCDs) were synthesized and then reacted with TEOS to generate NCDs@SiO2 according to the same method as mentioned above. As expected, NCDs@SiO2 can be stably dispersed in solution and generate blue fluorescence by UV irradiation. Surprisingly, a blue afterglow of NCDs@SiO2 aqueous solution was observed after UV excitation. We also used HR-TEM, SEM, FT-IR and XPS to characterize the nature of NCDS and NCDs@SiO2. Figure S10 shows that NCD@SiO2 are quasi-spherical, and NCDs are fixed in the silica network. FT-IR and XPS spectra imply the formation of covalent bonds between CDs and amorphous silica, as shown in Figure S11-12 . These characterizations indicate that the triplet excitons of NCDs are also well protected in amorphous silica through C-Si covalent bonds. Thus NCDs@SiO2 can be stably in solution without precipitation and produce afterglow.

As shown in Fig. 4
a, the PL spectra of NCDs@SiO2 solution is significantly blue-shifted compared with F, NCDs@SiO2. What’s more, under the optimal excitation, the absolute PL quantum yield of NCDs@SiO2 solution is determined to be 5.04%, which is much lower than F, NCDs@SiO2 (58.8%). To study the effect of fluorine doping on the afterglow performance of NCDs@SiO2, absorption, excitation and emission spectra were examined. Fig. 4 b reveals that the afterglow excitation spectra of NCDs@SiO2 coincides well with the absorption peak at 355 nm, which is ascribed to the n-π* transition of C = O/C = N. Although both afterglows come from the n-π* transition of C = O/C = N, the afterglow excitation wavelength of F, NCDs@SiO2 is red-shifted from 355 nm to 460 nm (Fig. 4 c) after the strong electron-withdrawing groups, fluorine, was doped. Therefore, the afterglow of NCDs@SiO2 solution cannot be excited by white light but UV can. The afterglow spectra of NCDs@SiO2 solution has two peaks at 420 nm and 470 nm, as shown in Fig. 4 d. We speculate that the afterglow may be composed of TADF and phosphorescence. To prove our deduction, temperature-dependent afterglow emission spectra were measured ( Figure S13
a).

As temperature rises, the afterglow emission at 420 nm gradually increases while that at 470 nm gradually decreases. Hence, there is indeed a TADF component with a thermal activation effect in the emission at 420 nm and a phosphorescent component at 470 nm. Fig. 4 e shows that the afterglow of NCDs@SiO2 is mainly composed of phosphorescence (about 70%) at room temperature by Gaussian fitting. Our speculation is further supported by the fact that the clear different afterglow lifetimes at 420 and 470 nm, i.e., 1.51 s and 1.92 s, respectively ( Figure S13
b). Meanwhile, the energy gap (ΔEST) can be quantitatively determined to be 0.57 eV based on the low temperature (77 K) fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of NCDs@SiO2 (Fig. 4 f). The energy level of NCDs@SiO2 is larger than that of F, NCDs@SiO2, which blocks the re-excitation of electrons from the T1 to the S1. So, the main component of the afterglow of NCDs@SiO2 is phosphorescence at room temperature. Therefore, the doping of fluorine groups plays a key role in obtaining visible-light excitable TADF in aqueous solution.

Based on the above analysis and discussion, the formation of F, NCDs@SiO2 and its special optical property mechanism can be tentatively proposed as follows. First, FT-IR and XPS show that F, CDs have many sub-fluorescent groups, such as –OH, –NH2 and C = O, which can play an important role in the generation of fluorescence and phosphorescence [46,47]. Meanwhile, the introduction of strong electronegative groups, fluorine, promotes electron transfer to reduce the energy level between HOMO and LUMO [25,28]. Therefore, the energy required for the transition of the electron to the excited state and the energy gaps between the S1 and the ground state (S0) is reduced, as shown in Fig. 4 g. As a result, both the excitation and emission wavelengths of the CDs tend to be red-shifted obtaining visible light excitation properties. Furthermore, a smaller ΔEST (0.32 eV) is obtained since the internal heavy atom effect of fluorine promotes ISC and SOC [30]. The reduction of ΔEST from 0.57 to 0.32 eV facilitates the process of reverse intersystem crossing to generate TADF by doping fluorine [12,35]. Therefore, the afterglow of F, NCDs@SiO2 solution is mainly TADF at room temperature. Secondly, the covalent bond and hydrogen network between amorphous silica and CDs effectively protect the triplet excitons of CDs from environmental quenchers and inhibit non-radiative transitions. More importantly, the large number of silanol groups on the surface of amorphous silica imparts good hydrophilicity to the composite. Briefly, owing to the fluorine doping and the protection by amorphous silica, we obtained the visible light-excited CDs-based TADF materials in aqueous solution.

Thanks to the visible light excitable characteristics and the rare green TADF, the prepared F, NCDs@SiO2 solution is considered to be used as an encryption security ink in the fields of advanced information encryption. For information encryption and decryption applications of the F, NCDs@SiO2, BCDs@SiO2 is used as interference. The BCDs@SiO2 produces blue fluorescence under ultraviolet excitation and produces green phosphorescence when the ultraviolet is turned off. The afterglow of BCDs@SiO2 cannot be excited by visible light. As shown in Fig. 5
a, we have encrypted the correct information (0105), the wrong information (8888) will be displayed under the UV lamp, and another group of error messages (8109) will appear when the UV lamp is turned off. The correct message (0105) appears only when the encrypted message has been illuminated by a cell phone LED. Additionally, we used blank markers with corresponding inks to depict several portraits with different movements (Fig. 5 b and
Supplementary Movie 2 ). Both the fluorescence image and the afterglow image show the small figures and their movement equipment by UV excitation. However, the afterglow images only show the small figures without sports equipment, when images were excited by cell phone LED. The cell phone LED is applied to the decryption process to improve the security of information and the convenience of decryption.

Because of the excellent biocompatibility, photostability and pH stability of F, NCDs@SiO2 (Fig. 6
a-c and Figure S14 ), we used human hepatocellular carcinoma (HepG2) cells to verify its afterglow imaging potential. As shown in Fig. 6 d, bright green fluorescence signals are observed in the HepG2 cells incubated with either F, NCDs@SiO2 or F, NCDs under white laser excitation. However, the fluorescence images of HepG2 cells containing F, NCDs or F, NCDs@SiO2 without time-delay exhibit relatively high background, while low background signal is noticed in the afterglow bioimaging of HepG2 cells containing F, NCDs@SiO2. Cells incubated with F, NCDs show no delayed fluorescence images due to the short fluorescence lifetimes. Moreover, F, NCDs@SiO2 with white light excitation is more valuable for afterglow bioimaging due to the less invasive and deeper tissue penetration compared with other CDs-based afterglow probes with UV excitation. The data cast light on the potential of F, NCDs@SiO2 as time-resolved probes for bioimaging with low background signal and high signal-to-noise ratio.

Given the TADF intensity of F, NCDs@SiO2 solution has a good correlation with temperature change when the temperature is changed from 303 K to 353 K, making this nanoparticle can be used as an ideal material for temperature sensing. The relationship can be described as follows:
(2)
I = 924.9 + 15.8 T

In this equation T stands for temperature (K) and it has a correlation coefficient of about 0.992. F, NCDs@SiO2 is sensitive to temperature changes as a temperature sensor in aqueous solution, making it promising for application to detect temperature changes in organisms at the cellular level.

In summary, as reported herein for the first time, visible light excitable CDs-based TADF material in aqueous solution was prepared by combining F, NCDs with silica nanoparticles. The amorphous silica effectively stiffens the triplet state of CDs while conferring good water solubility to the composite. As a result, F, N@SiO2 has a long-lived TADF of 0.48 s in aqueous solution. Further studies revealed that the energy gap between LUMO and HOMO is effectively reduced to redshift the excitation and emission wavelengths due to the electron-withdrawing effect of fluorine. The optimum afterglow excitation wavelength is red-shifted from 355 to 460 nm. Meanwhile, owing to the heavy atomic effect of fluorine, a significant decrease in ΔEST (from 0.57 to 0.32 eV) facilitates the reverse intersystem crossing between S1 and T1, generating TADF. Considering the property of visible-light-excited TADF, F, NCDs@SiO2 was effectively applied in double information encryption, temperature sensing and time-resolved fluorescence imaging. The delayed fluorescence image of HepG2 shows very low background noise. The results reported in this paper will promote the application of CDs in biological afterglow imaging and provide new avenues for the development of long-wavelength excited CDs-based afterglow materials.

Reagent grade of glycine, 2,4-difluorobenzoic, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and benzoic acid were bought from Aladdin Chemicals Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (pH = 7.4), and trypsin solutions were purchased from Thermo Fisher. The chemicals were used as received without further purification unless otherwise specified. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout this study.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were collected in a JEOL-2010 electron microscope. UV–Vis absorption spectrum was taken on a Shimadzu UV-2550 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. The X-ray photoelectron (XPS) was carried out on a Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi.hA. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) were measured by a Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence spectrum and afterglow decay curves were recorded with an F-7000 Hitachi fluorescence spectrofluorometer. The PL decay curves and the absolute quantum yield (QY) was measured using an FLS1000. Temperature dependent PL emission spectrum, afterglow emission spectrum, and afterglow decay curves were conducted by combining a heating apparatus (Oxford Instruments) with the same Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. We use commercial flashlight as an excitation light source for anti-counterfeiting applications (SMILING SHARK Y137). Confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) characterizations were conducted with a confocal laser scanning biological microscope (TCS SP8 STED 3X, LEICA). For excitation, a super-continuum white laser with 80 MHz pulse rate was used.

F, NCDs were prepared by a modified method [48]. 10 mmol 2,4-Difluorobenzoic acid and 13 mmol Glycine were dissolved in 50 mL DI-water and stirred for 30 min. Then the mixed solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the yellow supernatant was collected by suction filtration. The supernatant was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (500 Da) against water for 24 h. Finally, F, NCDs were obtained after freeze drying.

Briefly, F, NCDs (50 mg), deionized water (50 mL), and TEOS (8 mL) were added to a 100 mL beaker. The ammonia was then used to adjust the pH of the solution to 9. The mixed solution was transferred to a 100 mL flask and stirred at 100 ℃ for 5 h. The resulting product was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) against deionized water for 1 day. After filtering through a 0.22 μm pore sized membrane, the aqueous F, NCDs@SiO2 was obtained. The F, NCDs@SiO2 powers were collected by vacuum rotary evaporation at 65 ℃.

The human hepatocellular carcinoma (HepG2) cells was obtained from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC). HepG2 cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS). To investigate the cytotoxicity of the F, NCDs@SiO2, HepG2 cells were incubated with various concentrations of nanoparticles. The cell counting kit (CCK-8) assay was then used to measure the cell viability following cell incubation for 24 h.

HepG2 cells at a density of 2 × 105 cells/mL were seeded on a confocal dish (Ф: 35 mm, Nest brand). After 24 h of incubation, F, NCDs or F, NCDs@SiO2 solution (100 μg/mL) was added to the cell culture system, which was incubated for another 12 h. After washing thrice with PBS solution, the cells were directly measured by using laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscope (λex = 470 nm; emission was collected at 480–600 nm). The fluorescence images were collected when no time gating was set. Delayed fluorescence images were collected at time gating (8.5 ns-12 ns). A super-continuous white laser with a pulse frequency of 80 MHz was used as excitation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 21571067, 51402207, 11874021, and 32071399), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2020A1515010443), and the Science and Technology Program of Guangzhou (2019050001)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis method of NCDs@SiO2 and BCDs@SiO2. The SEM images, FL emission spectra, PL decay curves and TEM mapping of F, NCDs@SiO2. The XPS spectra, FT-IR spectra, TEM image, SEM image, afterglow decay curve and temperature- dependent afterglow spectra of NCDs@SiO2. Fluorescence stability spectra and suspension stability photos of F, NCDs@SiO2. Table S1 summarized reported work about carbon dots -based water-soluble phosphorescent complex. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.130728.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 2
